Glossary About
Machine Minds

Allen Newell & Herbert A. Simon

1927 – 1992 / 1916 – 2001

Symbol systems, problem solving, and the architecture of artificial intelligence.

Newell and Simon did not write a conventional “philosophy of mind” in the style of Descartes, Ryle, Putnam, Fodor, Searle, or Chalmers. Their philosophy is embedded in a scientific program: build symbol-processing systems; compare them with human problem-solving; use the resulting programs as precise theories of cognition; and generalize from them to claims about intelligence. Reconstructed philosophically, their view is a form of symbolic, physicalist, multi-level functionalism. It is ambitious about mind, intelligence, thought, problem-solving, knowledge, and the mind-body problem. It is much less complete about phenomenal consciousness, and Newell himself explicitly marks consciousness, distinguished from awareness, as a frontier problem not solved by Soar or by mainstream cognitive psychology.

The central finding is this: Newell and Simon mechanize a large part of what older metaphysics often assigned to mind or soul, but they do not actually supply a full theory of subjectivity, lived consciousness, or theological personhood. Their project is anti-mysterian about intelligence, anti-vitalist in tone, anti-Cartesian about the need for a nonphysical mental substance, and strongly committed to physically realizable cognitive organization. But it is not a complete metaphysics of the person.

1. The core doctrine: physical symbol systems

The basic thesis is the Physical Symbol System Hypothesis: a physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action. “Necessary” means that any generally intelligent system will, on analysis, be a physical symbol system; “sufficient” means that a physical symbol system of sufficient size and organization can exhibit general intelligence. Newell and Simon insist that this is not a theorem but an empirical hypothesis about the natural world, vulnerable to philosophical or scientific rejection if evidence goes against it.

A physical symbol system is “physical” in two senses: it obeys physics, and it is not restricted to human symbol use. It consists of physical patterns called symbols, symbol structures or expressions composed of such symbols, and processes that create, modify, reproduce, and destroy expressions over time. This definition matters philosophically because it relocates thought from a mysterious inner substance to a temporally extended, physically realized organization of symbol structures and operations.

Their account is not merely syntactic in the crude sense often attributed to “classical AI.” Newell and Simon define two central semantic relations: designation and interpretation. An expression designates an object when, given that expression, the system can affect the object or behave in ways dependent on it. A system interprets an expression when the expression designates a process that the system can carry out. Thus, for them, symbols are not inert marks; they are embedded in a system’s powers of access, action, and process execution.

This is why their philosophy of mind cannot be reduced to “the mind is just formal syntax.” It is better described as operational semantic functionalism: meaning is not a private glow attached to a symbol, nor a nonphysical mental act; it is a role in a system that can access objects, act on them, and execute procedures designated by expressions. That still leaves open whether such access is enough for genuine intentionality, but Newell and Simon’s own theory is more semantically ambitious than many later caricatures of symbolic AI.

2. No “vital principle,” no deus ex machina

The theological and quasi-theological language is not accidental. Newell and Simon explicitly reject the search for a single “intelligence principle,” comparing it to the abandoned idea of a “vital principle” in biology. They also say that the absence of a simple deus ex machina does not mean intelligence has no structural requirements; rather, one such requirement is the capacity to store and manipulate symbols.

This should be read carefully. They are not simply making an anti-religious aside. They are making a methodological and metaphysical move: intelligence is not explained by invoking an occult essence, but neither is it explained by denying structure. The replacement for “vital principle” is not nothing; it is a lawlike account of system organization. In this respect, their thought resembles the anti-vitalist turn in biology: life does not require a special life-substance, but living things still require determinate biological organization.

The theological implication is serious. If the “soul” is taken to mean an immaterial explanatory engine required to produce reasoning, problem-solving, knowledge-use, and intelligent action, then Newell and Simon’s project is directly opposed to it. If, however, “soul” means something like personhood, moral vocation, spiritual relation, immortality, or the theological depth of the human being, their work does not directly settle those questions. It undercuts a soul-of-the-gaps account of intelligence, not every possible doctrine of soul.

3. Programs as theories of mind, not metaphors

Newell, Shaw, and Simon’s 1958 theory of human problem-solving is crucial for understanding their philosophy. They present cognition as an information-processing control system involving memories, symbolized information, primitive information processes, and rules for combining those processes into programs. A program, in this context, is not a loose metaphor; it is a precise specification from which externally observable behavior can be deduced.

They explicitly deny that the computer is being used as a crude analogy to the brain. They say they are not comparing relays with synapses or computer structures with brain structures. Rather, their claim is that problem-solving behavior is properly described as a program: a specification of what an organism will do under varying environmental circumstances in terms of elementary information processes. They even compare a program’s relation to behavior with a differential equation’s relation to the physical system it describes.

This is one of the deepest philosophical commitments in their work. A theory of mind must be generative: it must produce the behavior it explains. Vague references to insight, intuition, judgment, or understanding are not enough unless they can be decomposed into definite processes. This is why they see computer simulation as scientifically transformative: it forces a theory of higher mental processes to become executable, testable, and revisable.

They also say that the mystery of problem-solving is dissolved when one shows how complex successes can be composed out of elementary information processes. The strong metaphysical conclusion is that, at least for problem-solving, nothing further needs to be added beyond appropriately organized mechanisms.

4. Intelligence as heuristic search under constraint

For Newell and Simon, intelligence is not perfect rationality, omniscience, or direct intellectual vision. It is bounded, procedural, and search-governed. Their Heuristic Search Hypothesis says that problem solutions are represented as symbol structures, and that a physical symbol system exercises intelligence by generating and progressively modifying symbol structures until it produces a solution structure.

This becomes a philosophy of finite mind. The intelligent system has limited resources; it cannot simply inspect all possibilities. It must choose what to do next. Newell and Simon define intelligence for such a system as making wise choices about the next step under conditions of scarcity. That is a profound shift: intelligence is not primarily possession of truths, but control of search in a structured space of possibilities.

Their treatment of Plato’s Meno is especially relevant to soul-language. Plato’s puzzle asks how inquiry is possible if one does not already know what one seeks; the Platonic answer invokes recollection, and therefore a metaphysical story of prior knowledge. Newell and Simon answer with a generator-test model: to state a problem is to specify a test for solutions and a generator of possible solutions; to solve it is to generate something satisfying the test. They explicitly say there is no need to invoke a previous existence or reincarnation.

This is one of their clearest anti-Platonic and anti-mystical moments. The “soul” need not have known the answer before birth; a symbol system can search. The metaphysical picture is therefore not anamnesis but constructive generation under constraints.

5. Mind-body: Simon’s boldest claim

Simon later described the Logic Theorist as showing that a system made of matter could exhibit properties of mind. A recent Springer article quotes Simon’s autobiographical claim that the Logic Theorist was “capable of thinking non-numerically” and thereby solved the venerable mind-body problem by explaining how matter could have properties of mind.

A French teaching document renders the same claim even more pointedly as the problem of “l’âme et du corps”—the soul and the body—saying that Newell and Simon’s program explained how a material system could exhibit properties of mind. This translation is philosophically revealing even if Simon’s English phrase was “mind/body”: in Francophone reception, the issue naturally shades into the older soul-body problematic.

The exact strength of Simon’s claim should be moderated. Logic Theorist did not solve consciousness, personhood, moral agency, or subjective experience. It did, however, provide a powerful demonstration of nonbiological symbolic reasoning. The philosophical lesson is that at least some capacities traditionally taken as marks of mind—proof search, theorem discovery, means-end reasoning, strategic selection—can be realized in matter without an immaterial mental substance.

So their mind-body solution is not neural identity theory. It does not say “mind is identical with brain tissue.” It says something closer to this: mind-like intelligent action is realized by the right physical organization of symbol-processing functions, and that organization can be implemented in different physical substrates. This is why their view belongs to the family of functionalism rather than reductive neuro-materialism.

6. The knowledge level: intentional realism without dualism

Newell’s later “knowledge level” is indispensable for extracting a mature philosophy of mind. He proposes a distinct system level above the symbol level. At this level, the system is an agent; its components are goals, actions, and body; its medium is knowledge; and its law of behavior is the principle of rationality: actions are selected to attain goals.

This is not eliminativism about intentional language. Newell does not say that talk of knowledge, goals, and rational action is merely folk illusion. He says system levels “really exist,” and he presents the knowledge level as a genuine scientific hypothesis. At the same time, he insists that the knowledge level can be reduced to symbol systems and that intelligent systems require both the knowledge level and the symbol level.

This yields a subtle metaphysics. The mental is not an extra substance, but neither is it dismissed as unreal. Knowledge, goals, and agency are real at their proper level of abstraction. Their reality is like the reality of other system levels: autonomous enough to support explanation, but dependent on lower-level realization.

Newell defines knowledge as closely linked with rationality and as a competence-like potential for generating action. Representations exist at the symbol level as data structures and processes that realize bodies of knowledge at the knowledge level. He summarizes representation with the formula: Representation = Knowledge + Access.

That formula is philosophically rich. It rejects the idea that knowledge is merely a stored inscription. A stored structure counts as knowledge only if there are processes that can extract it and use it in action selection. Knowledge is therefore dispositional, procedural, and action-oriented.

Newell is also alert to the homunculus danger. If one describes an internal component as if it were itself an intelligent agent, one risks simply hiding the mind inside the mind. Newell’s answer is that any knowledge-level description inside an actual system must ultimately be replaced by symbol-level descriptions.

So the mature Newellian philosophy is multi-level anti-homuncular intentional realism: intentional descriptions are legitimate, but they must not terminate in unexplained miniature agents.

7. Consciousness: the crucial limitation

Newell’s later work makes clear that he does not equate a theory of cognition with a complete theory of consciousness. He says a unified theory of cognition is by definition a theory of mind, but he also notes that a theory of mind is not, by definition, a theory of computational and informational processes; that computational presumption is the basic premise of cognitive science, shared by Soar.

He also emphasizes that architecture alone does not determine behavior. An architecture enables knowledge encoded in memory to determine behavior; that knowledge concerns goals, bodily state, task environment, social environment, and the physical world. This already complicates any simplistic reading in which mind is “just the program” in isolation from goals, body, and world.

The most important passage concerns consciousness. Newell says Soar provides a theory of awareness: the system is aware of something if its deliberate behavior can depend on it. But he distinguishes this operational awareness from consciousness understood as the “phenomenally subjective.” He says Soar does not touch consciousness in that sense, and that much of cognitive psychology does not either. He adds that the issue may be postponed until more regularities and phenomena accumulate, but “the challenge remains.”

This is decisive. Newell and Simon’s philosophy can explain a great deal about intelligence, problem-solving, knowledge, action, representation, and access-awareness. It does not contain a completed theory of phenomenal consciousness. If consciousness means reportability, functional accessibility, control relevance, or awareness-for-action, Newell has a route. If consciousness means what-it-is-like subjectivity, the theory remains incomplete by Newell’s own admission.

This also matters theologically. Many doctrines of soul are concerned less with problem-solving than with inwardness, subjectivity, moral selfhood, spiritual awareness, suffering, love, worship, and death. Newell and Simon’s theory does not directly explain these, although it may model some of their cognitive scaffolding.

8. The soul: four possible interpretations

The word “soul” must be disambiguated.

First, if soul means an occult explanatory principle needed to make reasoning possible, Newell and Simon reject it. Their anti-vitalist language, their generator-test answer to Plato, and their programmatic dissolution of the “mystery” of problem-solving all point in this direction.

Second, if soul means a Cartesian immaterial thinking substance required for intelligence, their view is incompatible with it. The sufficiency half of the Physical Symbol System Hypothesis says that a physical symbol system can be organized to exhibit general intelligent action. That leaves no necessary role for an immaterial cognitive substance in explaining intelligence.

Third, if soul means something closer to Aristotelian form—the organization by which a living or acting being has its characteristic powers—then a more interesting reconciliation becomes possible. Philip Agre’s essay “The Soul Gained and Lost” interprets Newell and Simon as offering a “mechanization of the soul”: not by locating the soul in a discrete internal device, but by treating it as manifested in the generative structure of search spaces and heuristic mechanisms. Agre explicitly notes an Aristotelian resonance: soul as form of the person rather than a detachable component.

Fourth, if soul means theological personhood—the human being as spiritual subject before God, bearer of moral responsibility, capable of grace, sin, worship, immortality, or eschatological destiny—then Newell and Simon do not give a direct theory. Their work may pressure such theology to stop using “soul” as a placeholder for unexplained cognition, but it does not by itself eliminate the theological category.

The strongest theological conflict arises when religious anthropology insists that intelligence and knowledge are intrinsically nonphysical. A 2024 article in TAFHIM: IKIM Journal of Islam and the Contemporary World frames the issue this way: computational theories of mind, including symbol-system approaches, treat intelligence and knowledge as physical, whereas the Islamic conception of the human nafs in the tradition the authors discuss treats the soul and the knowledge imprinted on it as nonphysical.

That is not a distraction from Newell and Simon; it is a direct metaphysical confrontation. Their view does not merely build useful machines. It proposes that a physically realized formal system can possess the means for general intelligent action. A theology that treats intelligence itself as necessarily rooted in a nonphysical soul must therefore reject either the necessity/sufficiency claim, or the interpretation of “intelligence” being used.

9. Non-Anglophone reception and contrasts

Several non-English sources bring out dimensions often muted in standard Anglo-American discussions.

In French reception, the soul-body resonance is especially explicit. The Picardie lecture document translates Simon’s autobiographical mind-body claim as the problem of “âme et corps,” then says the material system can exhibit properties of mind. It also emphasizes the role of heuristics in Logic Theorist: the issue is not applying formal rules blindly, but selecting which rules to apply and where, analogously to the mathematician choosing promising routes.

Jean-Louis Le Moigne, in a French article on “Symbols and Search,” treats Newell and Simon’s Turing lecture as an exceptional exercise in internal epistemological critique of computer science, one that introduces a solid theory of complex symbolization and legitimates Simon’s empiricist epistemology within a constructivist tradition. This French framing highlights something important: Newell and Simon are not merely proposing a machine model of mind; they are also proposing a way for computer science to become an empirical science of symbolization.

Another French source, in a CNRS/OpenEdition volume on phenomenology and cognitive science, says that neither Newell and Simon’s physical symbol system nor McCulloch and Pitts’ neural network is a model of the subject. It may be a model of mind in the English sense, but not of the subject; the author calls this “un esprit sans sujet,” a mind without subject. That is a sharp phenomenological diagnosis of the limitation we saw in Newell’s own distinction between awareness and phenomenal consciousness.

Spanish-language reception shows a similar tension. A Spanish educational chapter on physical symbol systems presents them as a materialist alternative to Cartesian dualism and states that classical cognitive science assumes cognition is rule-governed symbol manipulation of the kind a physical symbol system performs.

Ramón López de Mántaras, a Spanish AI researcher, criticizes the Physical Symbol System Hypothesis from a biological and consciousness-centered standpoint. He argues that AI systems are not conscious because they are not living beings, rejects substrate-indifference for consciousness, and says consciousness is deeply rooted in biology. His critique is not simply “machines cannot do tasks”; it targets the gap between symbolic or computational capacity and subjective experience.

A Spanish thesis on symbol systems in AI makes a more nuanced point: Newell and Simon’s symbols should not be dismissed as merely formal, because their system exists in a wider world of objects and includes designation. Yet the same work later argues, from a phenomenological angle, that symbolic systems do not obviously exhaust intelligence as lived consciousness. This is a useful corrective on both sides: Newell and Simon are not as semantically naive as critics sometimes suggest, but phenomenological consciousness remains a serious challenge.

A German discussion of Newell and Simon’s Turing lecture also stresses that they saw computer science as an empirical science: one translates intelligent human behavior into a theoretical model, realizes that model physically in a machine, and tests whether the machine behaves sufficiently like a human or diverges in ways that require revision.

Across these receptions, a pattern emerges. English-language philosophy often places Newell and Simon inside debates over functionalism, strong AI, Searle, and computationalism. French and Spanish discussions more readily foreground subjectivity, soul/body language, phenomenology, constructivist epistemology, and the difference between mind and subject. That difference is highly relevant to your purpose: Newell and Simon give us a theory of intelligent mind-as-processing, not a complete theory of the conscious subject.

10. What philosophy of mind can be extracted?

A comprehensive Newell-Simon philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as the following set of theses.

1. Physical realizability. Mental intelligence does not require a nonphysical substance. It requires a physically realizable organization capable of symbol manipulation, designation, interpretation, memory, and process execution.

2. Functional organization over substrate. The relevant explanatory level is not raw matter and not specifically neural tissue, but organized information-processing function. Humans and machines can instantiate relevantly similar processes at the symbol level.

3. Programs as psychological theories. A serious theory of mind must be executable or at least process-specific. It must show how behavior is generated, not merely redescribe it.

4. Anti-mystery, not anti-structure. Intelligence has no simple vital or divine spark as a scientific explanation, but it has structural requirements. Symbol systems are proposed as one such qualitative structural requirement.

5. Bounded rationality. Intelligence is not ideal omniscience. It is the ability of a finite system to guide search, exploit information, and choose effective next actions under resource constraints.

6. Problem spaces. Thinking is often movement through a represented space of possible states. Problem-solving consists in generating, transforming, testing, and selecting symbol structures.

7. Operational intentionality. Symbols mean by designation and interpretation: they connect system-internal structures to objects, actions, and executable processes.

8. Knowledge as action potential. Knowledge is not mere stored inscription. It is a competence-like potential to select actions in relation to goals.

9. Multi-level realism. Intentional descriptions at the knowledge level are real and scientifically useful, but they must be reducible in principle to symbol-level mechanisms. This avoids both crude reductionism and ungrounded homunculi.

10. Human-machine continuity. Human symbolic behavior is explained by the human being’s possession of the characteristics of a physical symbol system. Machines can, in principle, display intelligent action if organized appropriately.

11. Access-awareness functionalism. A system is aware of what its deliberate behavior can depend on. This is a theory of functional awareness, not a theory of phenomenal subjectivity.

12. Phenomenal incompleteness. The theory does not explain what-it-is-like consciousness. Newell explicitly acknowledges this limitation.

13. Soul deflation, not full theological negation. The theory deflates the soul as a necessary cognitive mechanism. It does not, by itself, settle doctrines of the soul as spiritual personhood, moral subject, or immortal being.

14. Personhood gap. The theory of the agent includes goals, actions, body, and knowledge, but it does not fully account for subjectivity, moral value, affect, religious experience, or the existential unity of a person. Newell’s own list of missing domains includes consciousness, dreaming, emotion and affect, motivation, and values and morals.

11. Critical assessment

The strength of Newell and Simon’s philosophy is that it refuses vague reverence for mind. It demands mechanisms. It converts “thinking” from a mysterious power into a class of physically realizable processes. It also gives us a sophisticated layered ontology: circuit level, symbol level, knowledge level, agent level. This layered view lets us say that knowledge and goals are real without making them ghostly substances.

Its weakness is that the same method works best where cognition is already task-like: theorem proving, puzzle solving, chess, planning, formal reasoning, and certain kinds of skill acquisition. The more one moves toward lived experience, affect, embodiment, intersubjectivity, moral selfhood, or religious consciousness, the less complete the account becomes. This is not an external accusation only; Newell’s own discussion of Soar admits that consciousness-as-phenomenality remains untouched.

The philosophy also faces the symbol-grounding and embodiment problem. Newell and Simon’s designation relation is stronger than mere syntax, but it still defines meaning operationally by access and action-dependence. A critic may ask whether such access is sufficient for understanding, or whether meaning also requires biological life, affective salience, perceptual embedding, social formation, or first-person acquaintance. Spanish and French phenomenological receptions press exactly this kind of objection.

Simon’s claim to have solved the mind-body problem is therefore best read as a dramatic but partial truth. Newell and Simon helped solve a major subproblem: how matter can perform formally organized, meaningful, intelligent reasoning without a Cartesian soul. They did not solve the full problem of consciousness, nor the theological problem of the soul, nor the phenomenological problem of the subject.

12. Final synthesis

The most faithful reconstruction is this:

For Newell and Simon, the mind is not an immaterial substance added to the body. It is a physically realized, symbol-using, knowledge-bearing, goal-directed information-processing organization. Thought is the controlled transformation of representations. Intelligence is the bounded ability to use knowledge to guide search and action. Meaning is grounded in a system’s capacity to designate objects and interpret expressions as executable processes. Knowledge and agency are real at a higher system level, but they must be realized by symbol-level mechanisms. The old “soul” as a mysterious cognitive engine is displaced by architecture, representation, and heuristic control.

But consciousness, in the deep phenomenal sense, remains outside the completed theory. Newell admits the distinction: Soar can model awareness as behavioral dependence, but not subjectivity. The result is therefore a powerful philosophy of intelligent mind, not a complete philosophy of conscious subjectivity. Theologically, it is a serious challenge to any doctrine that makes immaterial soul necessary for cognition; it is not a refutation of every doctrine that uses “soul” to name the spiritual, moral, or eschatological depth of the human person.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
